context: Current closing price of L3Harris (LHX): $353.59 USD as of 2026-04-10.
justification_text:
My point estimates across all time horizons (1 day, 1 week, and 1 month) are driven by an assumption that the central tendency of outcomes will remain stable over time, at least in the absence of any dramatic underlying shifts in the conditions influencing the variable being forecasted. The point estimate of 353.59 reflects the most likely outcome based on recent trends, historical averages, and other relevant data up to this point. I maintained the same point estimate across all horizons because, in this case, my base expectation is that the variable will tend to revert to a well-defined equilibrium or average.

The confidence interval widths, however, widen as the forecast horizon increases due to the accumulation of uncertainty over time. Over one day, there is relatively little time for substantial changes to occur, so the 90% confidence interval is relatively narrow (349.05 to 358.13). Over one week, the range broadens (343.25 to 363.93) because there is more time for variability and randomness to play a role. Similarly, by one month, the range becomes even wider (331.4 to 375.78) as the long-term nature of the forecast introduces additional uncertainty from factors like changing market conditions, external shocks, or trends that could deviate from expectations.

Specific risks or events could potentially push outcomes outside these intervals, particularly at the longer horizons. For example, unexpected, high-impact events—such as changes in government policies, major economic announcements, or significant geopolitical developments—could lead to outcomes well outside the estimated bounds. Additionally, if the variable being forecasted is influenced by heavily seasonal or cyclical patterns that I underestimated, the 90% confidence intervals may not fully capture those fluctuations. Finally, if there is a structural shift (such as a disrupted trend or significant shift in market dynamics), the model's assumptions would no longer apply, and actual outcomes could be far off from the forecast. All forecasts inherently rely on some level of stability in the underlying conditions, and when those conditions change, so does the reliability of the predictions.
